01_initial_request.target_name: Mixed-precision iterative refinement algorithm for sparse direct solvers
01_initial_request.input_data: Large-scale sparse linear systems from real-life industrial and academic applications
01_initial_request.user_intent: Accelerate the solution of large sparse linear systems by combining mixed-precision arithmetic with sparse factorizations
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Reduced computational cost and memory consumption with maintained numerical accuracy and scalability

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Sparse direct solvers for large linear systems face high computational cost and memory consumption. [Gap] Need to evaluate whether mixed-precision iterative refinement can accelerate sparse solvers while maintaining accuracy. [Decision] To achieve performance evaluation, chose to use MUMPS solver to perform sparse LU factorization on test matrices from SuiteSparse and industrial applications, expecting to obtain baseline performance metrics.

parameters.test_matrices: SuiteSparse Matrix Collection and industrial applications (ElectroPhys10M, DrivAer6M, tminlet3M, perf009ar, elasticity-3d, lfm_aug5M, CarBody25M, thmgas)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Baseline execution time and memory consumption recorded for DMUMPS (Double-precision MUMPS) on all test matrices with condition numbers computed for scaled matrices

references.citation: Amestoy et al., 2001, A fully asynchronous multifrontal solver using distributed dynamic scheduling
references.relevance: Provides foundation for MUMPS solver implementation used in experiments

references.citation: Davis and Hu, 2011, The university of florida sparse matrix collection
references.relevance: Source of test matrices from SuiteSparse collection

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Standard double-precision factorization provides baseline but high memory consumption. [Gap] Need to test if single-precision factorization with iterative refinement can reduce cost. [Decision] To achieve acceleration, chose to use LU-based iterative refinement (LU-IR) to perform factorization in single precision with double-precision solution, expecting to obtain reduced time and memory with maintained accuracy.

parameters.convergence_criterion: forward error equivalent to DMUMPS or 10^-16

02_agent_trajectory.observation: LU-IR variants with single-precision factorization achieved lowest execution times and memory consumption when converging, with up to 2× reduction compared to DMUMPS

references.citation: Langou et al., 2006; Buttari et al., 2007
references.relevance: Original work on mixed-precision iterative refinement for accelerating direct solvers

references.citation: Carson and Higham, 2018, Accelerating the solution of linear systems by iterative refinement in three precisions
references.relevance: Provides theoretical foundation for three-precision LU-IR variant

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] LU-IR may fail on ill-conditioned problems with low-precision factorization. [Gap] Need more robust method for moderately ill-conditioned matrices. [Decision] To achieve better robustness, chose to use GMRES-based iterative refinement (GMRES-IR) to perform preconditioned iterative solves, expecting to obtain convergence on more difficult problems.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: GMRES-IR variants more robust than LU-IR, converging on very ill-conditioned problems where LU-IR fails, but generally more expensive in execution time

references.citation: Carson and Higham, 2017, A new analysis of iterative refinement and its application to accurate solution of ill-conditioned sparse linear systems
references.relevance: Proposes GMRES-based iterative refinement for ill-conditioned problems

references.citation: Amestoy et al., 2021, Five Precision GMRES-based Iterative Refinement
references.relevance: Analyzes GMRES-IR in five precisions for flexible precision choices

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Standard LU factorization still has high complexity for large sparse problems. [Gap] Need to reduce operational complexity further through approximate factorizations. [Decision] To achieve additional savings, chose to use Block Low-Rank (BLR) factorization to perform low-rank compression of matrix blocks, expecting to obtain reduced factorization time and memory.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: BLR factorization with appropriate τ_b threshold achieved substantial reductions in execution time and memory, with optimal threshold depending on problem condition number

references.citation: Amestoy et al., 2015, Improving multifrontal methods by means of block low-rank representations
references.relevance: Introduces BLR format for sparse direct solvers

references.citation: Higham and Mary, 2021, Solving block low-rank linear systems by LU factorization is numerically stable
references.relevance: Provides error analysis for BLR LU factorization

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Partial pivoting reduces efficiency and scalability in parallel sparse factorizations. [Gap] Need alternative pivoting strategy that preserves sparsity and limits communications. [Decision] To achieve better parallelism, chose to use static pivoting to perform factorization without row/column permutations, expecting to obtain improved performance with acceptable accuracy loss recoverable by iterative refinement.

parameters.pivot_replacement: τ_s‖A‖_∞ for pivots smaller than threshold

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Static pivoting slightly accelerated factorization without excessively deteriorating convergence for many matrices, with optimal τ_s problem-dependent

references.citation: Li and Demmel, 1998, Making sparse gaussian elimination scalable by static pivoting
references.relevance: First proposed static pivoting for sparse systems

references.citation: Duff and Pralet, 2007, Towards stable mixed pivoting strategies
references.relevance: Discusses stability of static pivoting strategies

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] BLR and static pivoting can be combined for maximum approximation. [Gap] Need to evaluate combined effect on convergence and performance. [Decision] To achieve maximum savings, chose to use combined BLR with static pivoting to perform factorization with both approximations, expecting to obtain largest time and memory reductions.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Combined BLR and static pivoting achieved larger reductions than either alone, with convergence conditions affected by max(τ_s, τ_b) and growth factor ρ_n

references.citation: Amestoy et al., 2019, Performance and scalability of the block low-rank multifrontal factorization on multicore architectures
references.relevance: Describes BLR implementation in MUMPS that supports combination with static pivoting

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Theoretical error analysis needed for approximate factorizations with iterative refinement. [Gap] Standard convergence bounds assume partial pivoting, not applicable to approximate methods. [Decision] To achieve theoretical understanding, chose to use general approximate factorization error analysis to derive convergence conditions for LU-IR and GMRES-IR, expecting to obtain bounds accounting for approximation error ε and growth factor ρ_n.

parameters.approximation_parameter: ε (τ_b for BLR, τ_s for static pivoting, τ_b+τ_s for combined)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Derived Theorem 4.1 showing convergence conditions include terms ρ_n·u_f + ε, confirming instabilities from element growth and approximations combine additively

references.citation: Carson and Higham, 2018, Accelerating the solution of linear systems by iterative refinement in three precisions
references.relevance: Provides base error analysis extended to approximate factorizations

references.citation: Amestoy et al., 2021, Five Precision GMRES-based Iterative Refinement
references.relevance: Provides five-precision analysis framework adapted for this work

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Need to validate overall performance improvements across all variants. [Gap] Need comprehensive comparison of best configurations. [Decision] To achieve final validation, chose to use performance measurement to compare best execution time and memory consumption amongst all iterative refinement variants, expecting to obtain quantified speedup and memory reduction factors.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Obtained up to 5.6× reduction in time (lfm_aug5M) and 4.4× reduction in memory (thmgas) compared to double-precision solver while preserving double-precision accuracy

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Performance benchmarking on large-scale sparse problems from real-life industrial and academic applications with forward error comparison to double-precision baseline

time_reduction.interpretation: Maximum execution time reduction achieved on lfm_aug5M matrix compared to DMUMPS baseline

memory_reduction.interpretation: Maximum memory consumption reduction achieved on thmgas matrix compared to DMUMPS baseline

accuracy_maintenance.interpretation: All variants stopped when reaching forward error equivalent to double-precision direct solver DMUMPS

typical_speedup.interpretation: LU-IR with standard single-precision factorization typically reduces time and memory by up to 2× compared to double-precision solver

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was successfully achieved. Mixed-precision iterative refinement combined with approximate sparse factorizations (BLR and static pivoting) demonstrated considerable reductions in both computational time (up to 5.6×) and memory consumption (up to 4.4×) while maintaining double-precision numerical accuracy on large-scale sparse linear systems from real-life industrial and academic applications.
